Technip Energies (PARIS:TE) has been awarded a substantial1 contract by SkyNRG for the DSL-01 project, a new Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) production facility to be developed in Delfzijl, the Netherlands.
This contract represents Technip Energies’ sixth HEFA-based SAF project in Europe, reinforcing its position as a key enabler for sustainable aviation fuel capacities in the region.
Sylvain Cabalery, Head of Commercial, Project Delivery & Services business unit at Technip Energies, stated: We are pleased to have been entrusted by SkyNRG to deliver this important sustainable aviation fuel project.
Technip Energies has delivered more than 60% of the installed SAF production capacity globally.
”Together with Technip Energies as our trusted and expert EPCC partner, we can now turn years of development into a physical facility.
text:
Technip Energies (PARIS:TE) has been awarded a substantial1 contract by SkyNRG for the DSL-01 project, a new Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) production facility to be developed in Delfzijl, the Netherlands.

The award covers Engineering, Procurement, Construction and Commissioning (EPCC) for Europe’s first standalone greenfield SAF production facility. It follows the successful completion of the Front-End Engineering & Design (FEED) phase by Technip Energies and marks a significant step in the long-standing collaboration with SkyNRG.

The DSL-01 project is designed to produce 100,000 tons per annum of SAF using the Hydroprocessed Esters and Fatty Acids (HEFA) pathway, processing sustainable waste feedstocks such as used cooking oil.

The project integrates an advanced feedstock pre-treatment unit and an on-site hydrogen plant based on Technip Energies’ proprietary Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) technology. This configuration is intended to improve cost efficiency and minimize lifecycle emissions, thereby enabling large-scale competitive production of SAF.

This contract represents Technip Energies’ sixth HEFA-based SAF project in Europe, reinforcing its position as a key enabler for sustainable aviation fuel capacities in the region.

Sylvain Cabalery, Head of Commercial, Project Delivery & Services business unit at Technip Energies, stated:

We are pleased to have been entrusted by SkyNRG to deliver this important sustainable aviation fuel project.

”With a planned capacity of 100,000 tons per year, this initiative marks a significant advancement in scaling SAF production across Europe. Technip Energies has delivered more than 60% of the installed SAF production capacity globally. This award reinforces our leadership in this strategic market and underscores our continued commitment towards a more sustainable future.”

Maarten van Dijk, Chief Executive Officer and Co-founder of SkyNRG, stated:

Reaching this milestone for DSL-01 is a defining step in our journey toward operating our own SAF production capacity.

”Together with Technip Energies as our trusted and expert EPCC partner, we can now turn years of development into a physical facility. Scaling SAF is essential for the future of aviation, and this project is a concrete contribution to its decarbonization.”

(1) A “substantial” award for Technip Energies is a contract award representing between €500 million and €1 billion of revenue. This award was recorded in Q1 2026 in the Project Delivery and Technology, Products & Services segments.

title: Europe Aircraft Market Size, Share & Growth, 2034
authors: Market Data Forecast, Market Data Forecast ltd
description:
Europe Aircraft Market Size The size of the Europe aircraft market was worth USD 134 billion in 2025.
COUNTRY-LEVEL ANALYSIS France Aircraft Market Analysis France outperformed other countries in the Europe aircraft market by accounting for a 28.4% share in 2025.
Germany Aircraft Market Analysis Germany was the second largest country in the Europe aircraft market by holding a 22.3% share in 2025.

By Type Commercial Aircraft Passenger Aircraft Freighter Military Aircraft Combat Aircraft Non-Combat Aircraft By Size Narrow Body Aircraft Wide Body Aircraft By Country
text:
Europe Aircraft Market Size

The size of the Europe aircraft market was worth USD 134 billion in 2025. The regional market is anticipated to grow at a CAGR of 2.74% from 2026 to 2034 and be worth USD 170.89 billion by 2034 from USD 137.66 billion in 2026.

An aircraft is any machine or vehicle designed for flight in the atmosphere. Unlike markets driven primarily by domestic demand, Europe’s aircraft sector functions as a strategic export engine and technological leader, with Airbus alone accounting for a significant share of global commercial jet deliveries. The European aerospace and defense industry represents a vital, high-growth, and high-skilled advanced manufacturing cluster, driven by significant turnover in civil and military aeronautics along with space, with total employment and revenues continuing to rise. The market operates under stringent regulatory oversight from the European Union Aviation Safety Agency, which sets global benchmarks for certification, safety, and environmental performance. Critically, Europe is at the forefront of aviation decarbonization. Furthermore, the European aviation sector is collectively committed to a net-zero carbon future by mid-century through a collaborative roadmap, intensifying investments in sustainable aviation fuels, hydrogen technology, and improved operational efficiency. This fusion of industrial might, regulatory leadership, and climate ambition defines the European aircraft market as a cornerstone of both economic sovereignty and global aerospace innovation.

MARKET DRIVERS

Europe’s Leadership in Commercial Aircraft Manufacturing and Export

The region’s dominance in global commercial aircraft production, primarily through Airbus, serves as the primary driver of the Europe aircraft market. From its primary base in France, Airbus continues to lead the international aviation sector by delivering a majority of the world's new commercial jetliners. The A320neo series remains the preferred choice for major European airline groups looking to modernize their narrow-body fleets with more fuel-efficient technology. The company maintains an integrated manufacturing network across Europe, distributing specialized production and assembly tasks among various nations while supporting an extensive ecosystem of smaller suppliers. Aerospace manufacturing, led by aircraft production, stands as the most significant contributor to high-value industrial exports for the European economy. This manufacturing ecosystem not only generates high-value jobs but also anchors R&D in materials science, avionics, and aerodynamics, reinforcing Europe’s position as a net exporter of aerospace technology and a counterbalance to U.S. dominance.

EU Climate Policy and the Push for Sustainable Aviation Innovation

The European Union’s aggressive decarbonization agenda is a powerful structural force reshaping aircraft demand toward next-generation and low-emission platforms, which also boosts the expansion of the Europe aircraft market. The European Union is implementing mandatory, rising quotas for sustainable aviation fuels at EU airports, requiring a steady increase in green fuel blending to significantly decrease emissions, with a heavy emphasis on advanced and synthetic fuels by mid-century. Moreover, the European Environment Agency confirms that aviation remains a major, fast-growing contributor to EU transport emissions, increasing pressure on the sector to transition toward sustainable operations. In response, Airbus has fast-tracked its ZEROe program, targeting hydrogen-powered aircraft entry into service by 2035. Simultaneously, airlines are accelerating fleet renewal. IAG is reducing its overall fuel consumption and environmental footprint by replacing older aircraft with more modern, efficient single-aisle jets as part of a long-term commitment to sustainability. This policy-driven transition ensures sustained demand for new, efficient aircraft while positioning Europe as the global testbed for zero-emission flight technologies.

MARKET RESTRAINTS

Stringent Certification and Regulatory Delays

The rigorous and time-intensive certification processes mandated by the European Union Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) are a major restraint on the Europe aircraft market. EASA’s rigorous safety standards are instrumental for industry safety, yet they frequently create prolonged approval processes that hinder market entry and raise development costs. Regulatory approval for new aircraft models is becoming more time-intensive as authorities prioritize complex, modern safety requirements. Concurrently, the development of new, long-range narrow-body aircraft is experiencing delays as manufacturers work to satisfy strict, specialized fuel storage safety regulations. These delays disrupt airline fleet planning, increase financing costs, and erode competitive advantage against faster-certifying rivals. For startups and SMEs developing urban air mobility or hydrogen aircraft, the regulatory burden is even more prohibitive, stifling innovation despite strong policy support for clean aviation.

High Development Costs and Supply Chain Fragility

The astronomical capital intensity of aircraft development and persistent supply chain vulnerabilities pose significant obstructions to market agility and competitiveness, which in turn hinder the expansion of the Europe aircraft market. Launching a new commercial aircraft program necessitates substantial, long-term financial investment, commonly exceeding prior industry cost benchmarks and requiring many years to achieve profitability. This risk deters all but the largest players, limiting competition and innovation. Compounding this, the pandemic exposed deep fragility in the aerospace supply chain. In the wake of the pandemic, lower-tier aerospace suppliers faced severe liquidity and operational challenges, resulting in significant bottlenecks for critical components and materials. Geopolitical tensions further strain sourcing. Sanctions on Russian titanium, a key lightweight alloy, forced redesigns and cost hikes. The vulnerability of the European aircraft market to external shocks persists, hindering fleet renewal and jeopardizing production timelines in the absence of stronger supply chain resilience and collaborative risk management.

MARKET OPPORTUNITIES

Pioneering Hydrogen-Powered Regional Aviation

The region’s command in developing hydrogen-powered aircraft for short-haul routes aligns with the EU’s net zero aviation goals and uncovers new paths for the Europe aircraft market. The European Union is directing substantial funding through public-private partnerships to advance hydrogen-fueled propulsion systems, supporting major manufacturer demonstrators and regional aviation efforts to accelerate zero-emission flight. European policy highlights that regional and short-haul flights are optimal for adopting hydrogen technology, as this shift offers a pathway to fully eliminate carbon dioxide and significantly mitigate contrail formation in aviation. Startups like Universal Hydrogen have partnered with European carriers such as DAT to retrofit turboprops, targeting entry into service. Airports like Rotterdam and Toulouse are building liquid hydrogen refueling infrastructure, supported by EU Innovation Fund grants. Targeting regional, non-HSR-served markets allows Europe to secure an early advantage in zero-emission flight, setting the stage for global technology export.

Expansion of Urban Air Mobility and Advanced Air Mobility Ecosystems

The region’s coordinated push to integrate electric vertical take-off and landing (eVTOL) aircraft into urban transport networks expands the scope for the European aircraft market. Europe is implementing a standardized digital airspace management framework that enables automated, low-altitude drone operations, with initial operational capabilities for air taxis expected in the coming years. European cities are actively preparing for the launch of electric air taxi services, with manufacturers pursuing rigorous EASA safety certifications to bring piloted, low-noise passenger aircraft into commercial service. Significant public funding and research initiatives are being directed by European authorities toward developing the technology, infrastructure, and regulatory frameworks required for the widespread adoption of urban air mobility. These aircraft promise to cut urban commute times while producing near-zero noise and emissions. Europe is setting the international standard for safe, scalable urban air mobility through integrated air traffic management and new vertiport infrastructure, driving a high-tech export sector that extends beyond traditional aviation.

MARKET CHALLENGES

Geopolitical Fragmentation in Defense and Industrial Policy

The lack of unified defense and industrial strategy among member states undermines economies of scale and global competitiveness, and therefore holds back the growth of the Europe aircraft market. Despite initiatives like the Future Combat Air System (FCAS) and Global Combat Air Programme (GCAP), national preferences often override collaboration. Germany and Spain favor FCAS, while the UK leads GCAP with Japan and Italy, duplicating R&D efforts. According to sources, the fragmented nature of European military procurement results in a much higher variety of active combat aircraft types compared to the more consolidated, streamlined inventory of the United States, driving up maintenance and support expenses. This trend reflects inefficient, fragmented, and cost-inflated sustainment across European air forces. This fragmentation weakens bargaining power against U.S. and Chinese competitors and slows the adoption of common platforms. Europe's failure to harmonize aerospace regulations, buying policies, and export controls threatens to undermine its long-term strategic independence in both civilian and defense sectors.

Workforce Shortage in Critical Aerospace Disciplines

A severe human capital crisis, with critical shortages of engineers, technicians, and digital specialists, hinders the production, innovation, and expansion of the Europe aircraft market. The European aerospace and defence sector faces a massive, impending workforce crisis driven by an aging employee demographic, necessitating a massive influx of new talent to meet future production and innovation goals. In addition, the French aerospace industry is struggling to replace a large portion of its experienced workforce nearing retirement, while German composite manufacturing, a critical part of aerospace production, is experiencing significant vacancies due to a lack of specialized skilled workers. The shift to digital engineering, hydrogen systems, and AI-driven maintenance demands new competencies that traditional curricula do not provide. Europe’s industrial leadership and climate goals are at risk unless it implements targeted education reforms, expands apprenticeships, and creates immigration pathways for technical experts. Without these, the continent will struggle to develop next-generation aircraft.

REPORT COVERAGE

SEGMENTAL ANALYSIS

By Type Insights

The commercial aircraft segment dominated the Europe aircraft market by accounting for a substantial share in 2025. The dominance of the commercial aircraft segment is driven by Europe’s position as a global hub for civil aviation manufacturing, primarily through Airbus, which delivered a large number of commercial jets in 2023, capturing over half of the global market. Airbus dominates the European single-aisle aircraft market, with its A320neo family securing a massive majority of new orders as major network carriers and low-cost carriers aggressively modernize their fleets with more efficient, higher-capacity models. The segment is further reinforced by the EU’s “Destination 2050” sustainability roadmap, which mandates fleet renewal to meet net zero emissions by 2050, accelerating demand for fuel-efficient models like the A320neo and A220. Additionally, passenger traffic at European airports has fully recovered to exceed pre-pandemic highs, driving sustained demand for narrow-body aircraft for short- and medium-haul routes, with significant growth in international leisure and visiting-friends-and-relatives (VFR) travel. This combination of industrial scale, regulatory pressure, and traffic recovery ensures commercial aviation remains the economic core of Europe’s aerospace sector.

The military aircraft segment is anticipated to witness the fastest CAGR of 7.4% from 2026 to 2034 due to heightened defense spending following Russia’s invasion of Ukraine and NATO’s call for members to allocate at least 2% of GDP to defense. European Union member states have significantly increased collective defense spending and investment in equipment. Major procurement trends include France expanding its fighter fleet, Germany investing in stealth aircraft, and Spain enhancing its air capabilities with advanced European fighters. The Future Combat Air System (FCAS) and Global Combat Air Programme (GCAP), jointly developed by France, Germany, Spain, the UK, Italy, and Japan, are injecting billions into next-generation stealth and unmanned platforms. Furthermore, the EU’s Strategic Compass emphasizes air superiority and rapid deployment, driving demand for multirole fighters, transport aircraft, and surveillance drones. This geopolitical realignment transforms military aviation from a stable niche into a high-growth pillar of Europe’s aerospace future.

By Size Insights

In 2025, the narrow-body aircraft segment led the Europe aircraft market by holding a significant share because of its optimal fit for Europe’s dense network of short- and medium-haul routes, which account for a significant share of intra-EU air traffic. Airlines across Europe are increasingly adopting modern, fuel-efficient narrow-body aircraft to operate high-frequency, short-haul routes, replacing older models to enhance sustainability and profitability on busy European city pairs. The Airbus A320neo family has seen massive order uptake from European airlines and leasing firms, driven by substantial reductions in fuel consumption and the necessity to comply with stricter EU environmental and emissions regulations. Low-cost carriers like Ryanair and Wizz Air operate exclusively narrow-body fleets, with Ryanair’s order book exceeding 300 A321neos. This operational and economic alignment with Europe’s aviation geography ensures narrow bodies remain the backbone of regional air mobility and airline profitability.

The wide-body aircraft segment is likely to experience the fastest CAGR of 6.8% over the forecast period, owing to the recovery of long-haul international travel and strategic fleet modernization by flag carriers. According to the International Air Transport Association, European-wide-body capacity on intercontinental routes experienced a strong recovery in 2023 compared to 2019 levels, with key markets like North America and Asia driving significant growth as international travel demand rebounded. Airlines such as Lufthansa, Air France, and British Airways are replacing older Boeing 747s and Airbus A340s with fuel-efficient A350s and Boeing 787s, reducing CO₂ emissions. The EU’s ReFuelEU mandate also incentivizes newer wide bodies capable of using 100% sustainable aviation fuel. Additionally, cargo demand, boosted by e-commerce, has revived interest in freighters like the A330-200F. As Europe reasserts its global connectivity, wide bodies are regaining strategic importance beyond passenger comfort, serving as critical enablers of trade and tourism.

COUNTRY-LEVEL ANALYSIS

France Aircraft Market Analysis

France outperformed other countries in the Europe aircraft market by accounting for a 28.4% share in 2025. The dominance of the French market stems from being home to Airbus headquarters, Dassault Aviation, and Safran, a vertically integrated ecosystem spanning airframes, engines, and avionics. The French government is accelerating military aviation modernization by funding the production of new-generation Rafale fighters and investing heavily in the development of next-generation, collaborative European air combat systems. Civil aviation thrives through Air France’s fleet renewal and Toulouse’s status as Europe’s aerospace capital, hosting a significant number of skilled workers. Through its national investment initiative, France is prioritizing the development of sustainable aeronautics, allocating substantial funding to accelerate research, development, and industrialization of electric, hybrid, and hydrogen-powered aircraft. This fusion of industrial policy, defense sovereignty, and climate innovation cements France as the strategic nucleus of European aerospace.

Germany Aircraft Market Analysis

Germany was the second largest country in the Europe aircraft market by holding a 22.3% share in 2025. The expansion of the German market is driven by its advanced manufacturing base and engineering excellence. The country hosts major Airbus final assembly lines in Hamburg and engine production by MTU Aero Engines, employing a large number of aerospace professionals. Germany is upgrading its air force by purchasing advanced US stealth fighters and additional European-made jets, funded by a dedicated national defense initiative. These acquisitions, initiated in 2022 for the F-35s and expanded in 2024 for the Eurofighters, aim to modernize the fleet and bolster long-term capabilities. Germany also leads in digital aviation. The German Aerospace Center (DLR) operates Europe’s largest hydrogen propulsion test facility. The national “H2Airport” initiative aims to make Hamburg a hub for hydrogen refueling by 2030. This blend of industrial scale, defense modernization, and green technology positions Germany as the engineering backbone of European aviation.

United Kingdom Aircraft Market Analysis

The United Kingdom maintains a noteworthy position in the European aircraft market due to defense aviation and advanced R&D. Home to BAE Systems, Rolls-Royce, and Marshall Aerospace, the UK plays a leading role in the Global Combat Air Programme and produces the Eurofighter Typhoon. The UK Ministry of Defence is directing significant long-term funding toward next-generation combat air systems, bolstering the domestic aerospace sector and sustaining specialized engineering employment across the country. Rolls-Royce maintains a substantial market presence in large-engine aviation and is testing new technology that significantly improves fuel efficiency compared to its previous industry-standard engines. The UK also pioneers urban air mobility through Vertical Aerospace and the ATI’s FlyZero hydrogen project. Despite Brexit, the UK maintains deep technical integration with European programs, ensuring its continued influence as a center of aerospace innovation and sovereign capability.

Spain Aircraft Market Analysis

Spain witnessed a consistent growth in the Europe aircraft market by serving as a critical Airbus production site and emerging defense exporter. Airbus facilities in Getafe and Illescas serve as critical hubs for producing major military transport components and advanced commercial tail assemblies, supporting a massive specialized workforce. The Spanish government is significantly increasing its financial commitment to European-led defense initiatives, specifically prioritizing domestic fighter jet production and next-generation combat systems over international alternatives. Spain’s strategic location enables efficient export logistics to Latin America and North Africa, with Airbus Helicopters’ Marignane plant supplying H135s regionally. Spain’s primary strategic aerospace initiative is driving industry-wide transitions toward ecological sustainability and digital innovation by funding collaborative research and strengthening smaller suppliers. This combination of industrial anchoring, defense investment, and geographic advantage establishes Spain as a rising force in European aerospace manufacturing.

Italy Aircraft Market Analysis

Italy is likely to expand in the Europe aircraft market from 2026 to 2034 due to its dual role in military collaboration and regional aviation. Leonardo S.p.A. produces AW139 helicopters and M-346 trainer jets, while Alenia Aeronautica manufactures ATR turboprops and A320 fuselages. The Italian Ministry of Defence is expanding its fifth-generation fighter fleet by purchasing additional F-35 aircraft while strengthening international defense ties through its leadership role in the collaborative sixth-generation GCAP project with the United Kingdom and Japan. In addition, the ATR joint venture, co-owned by Leonardo and Airbus, continues to supply regional turboprop aircraft to airlines, maintaining its focus on connecting secondary airports across Europe and the globe. Italy is utilizing its national recovery funds to accelerate the decarbonization of the aerospace sector, investing heavily in infrastructure for hydrogen propulsion and sustainable aviation fuels. This balance of industrial partnership, defense interoperability, and regional connectivity ensures Italy remains a versatile and influential player in the European aerospace landscape.

COMPETITIVE LANDSCAPE

The Europe aircraft market features intense competition between integrated giants like Airbus and specialized defense leaders such as BAE Systems and Dassault Aviation. Commercial aviation is dominated by Airbus, which competes globally with Boeing, while regional players focus on niche segments like business jets and military trainers. The market is increasingly shaped by climate policy, with sustainability becoming a key differentiator in both civil and defense procurement. Fragmentation persists in military programs due to national preferences despite collaborative frameworks like FCAS and GCAP. Supply chain vulnerabilities, workforce shortages, and certification delays add operational risk. Success hinges on balancing industrial scale with technological leadership in a landscape where regulatory compliance, strategic autonomy, and environmental performance are equally critical.

KEY MARKET PLAYERS

The leading companies operating in the Europe aircraft market include:

Boeing Company

Dassault Aviation SA

Airbus SE

BAE Systems plc

Lockheed Martin Corporation

Textron Inc.

Embraer S.A.

TOP PLAYERS IN THE MARKET

Airbus SE is a European multinational aerospace corporation and the continent’s flagship aircraft manufacturer, headquartered in Toulouse France. The company designs and produces commercial, military, and business jets that serve airlines and governments worldwide. Airbus has strengthened its position by accelerating the ramp-up of A320neo and A350 production to meet post-pandemic demand while advancing its ZEROe hydrogen aircraft program targeting entry into service. It also expanded digital engineering capabilities using artificial intelligence for predictive maintenance and fuel efficiency optimization. These initiatives reinforce Airbus’s leadership in sustainable aviation and ensure its continued influence on global aerospace standards and decarbonization pathways.

BAE Systems plc is a British defense aerospace and security company that plays a pivotal role in Europe’s military aviation sector through combat aircraft, naval systems, and advanced electronics. The company contributes significantly to the Global Combat Air Programme, which aims to deliver a sixth-generation fighter by 2035. BAE has reinforced its market presence by investing in digital twin technology for Typhoon fleet sustainment and by developing autonomous loyal wingman drones to complement manned platforms. It also partners with UK universities to address aerospace workforce shortages through specialized engineering programs. These actions position BAE as a cornerstone of European defense sovereignty and next generation air combat innovation.

Dassault Aviation SA is a French aerospace company renowned for its Rafale multirole fighter and Falcon business jets, which are exported globally. The company maintains a strong presence in both defense and civil segments with a focus on technological excellence and operational sovereignty. Dassault has strengthened its position by upgrading the Rafale F5 standard with AI-enabled mission systems and by advancing the Falcon 6X with enhanced fuel efficiency and cabin comfort. It also participates in the Future Combat Air System alongside Airbus and Indra to develop next-generation European air dominance capabilities. These strategies ensure Dassault remains a key player in high end aviation with deep integration into national and European strategic priorities.

TOP STRATEGIES USED BY THE KEY MARKET PARTICIPANTS

Key players in the Europe aircraft market are primarily focused on accelerating fleet renewal with fuel-efficient aircraft to comply with EU emissions regulations and meet airline demand. Companies are investing heavily in hydrogen and sustainable aviation fuel technologies to achieve net-zero goals by 2050. Strategic collaboration on next-generation combat air systems like FCAS and GCAP ensures defense sovereignty and cost-sharing. Digital transformation through artificial intelligence, digital twins, and predictive maintenance enhances operational efficiency and reduces lifecycle costs. Additionally, firms are expanding supply chain resilience and workforce development programs to address bottlenecks and secure long-term industrial capacity in a geopolitically volatile environment.

title: Technip Energies awarded a substantial contract for SkyNRG's Sustainable Aviation Fuel ...
description:
Technip Energies (PARIS:TE) has been awarded a substantial[1] contract by SkyNRG for the DSL-01 project, a new Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) production facility to be developed in Delfzijl, the Netherlands.
The project integrates an advanced feedstock pre-treatment unit and an on-site hydrogen plant based on Technip Energies’ proprietary Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) technology.
This contract represents Technip Energies' sixth HEFA-based SAF project in Europe, reinforcing its position as a key enabler for sustainable aviation fuel capacities in the region.
Together with Technip Energies as our trusted and expert EPCC partner, we can now turn years of development into a physical facility.
Technip Energies generated revenues of €6.9 billion in 2024 and is listed on Euronext Paris.
text:
Technip Energies (PARIS:TE) has been awarded a substantial[1] contract by SkyNRG for the DSL-01 project, a new Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) production facility to be developed in Delfzijl, the Netherlands.

The award covers Engineering, Procurement, Construction and Commissioning (EPCC) for Europe’s first standalone greenfield SAF production facility. It follows the successful completion of the Front-End Engineering & Design (FEED) phase by Technip Energies and marks a significant step in the long-standing collaboration with SkyNRG.

The DSL-01 project is designed to produce 100,000 tons per annum of SAF using the Hydroprocessed Esters and Fatty Acids (HEFA) pathway, processing sustainable waste feedstocks such as used cooking oil.

The project integrates an advanced feedstock pre-treatment unit and an on-site hydrogen plant based on Technip Energies’ proprietary Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) technology. This configuration is intended to improve cost efficiency and minimize lifecycle emissions, thereby enabling large-scale competitive production of SAF.

This contract represents Technip Energies' sixth HEFA-based SAF project in Europe, reinforcing its position as a key enabler for sustainable aviation fuel capacities in the region.

Sylvain Cabalery, Head of Commercial, Project Delivery & Services business unit at Technip Energies, stated: “We are pleased to have been entrusted by SkyNRG to deliver this important sustainable aviation fuel project. With a planned capacity of 100,000 tons per year, this initiative marks a significant advancement in scaling SAF production across Europe. Technip Energies has delivered more than 60% of the installed SAF production capacity globally. This award reinforces our leadership in this strategic market and underscores our continued commitment towards a more sustainable future.”

Maarten van Dijk, Chief Executive Officer and Co-founder of SkyNRG, stated: “Reaching this milestone for DSL-01 is a defining step in our journey toward operating our own SAF production capacity. Together with Technip Energies as our trusted and expert EPCC partner, we can now turn years of development into a physical facility. Scaling SAF is essential for the future of aviation, and this project is a concrete contribution to its decarbonization.”

[1] A “substantial” award for Technip Energies is a contract award representing between €500 million and €1 billion of revenue. This award was recorded in Q1 2026 in the Project Delivery and Technology, Products & Services segments.

Technip Energies is a global technology and engineering powerhouse. With leadership positions in LNG, hydrogen, ethylene, sustainable chemistry, and CO2 management, we are contributing to the development of critical markets such as energy, energy derivatives, decarbonization, and circularity. Our complementary business segments, Technology, Products and Services (TPS) and Project Delivery, turn innovation into scalable and industrial reality.

Through collaboration and excellence in execution, our 17,000+ employees across 34 countries are fully committed to bridging prosperity with sustainability for a world designed to last.

Technip Energies generated revenues of €6.9 billion in 2024 and is listed on Euronext Paris. The Company also has American Depositary Receipts trading over the counter.

For further information: www.ten.com

outlet: Kuehne+Nagel, LATAM Cargo, and The Elite Flower carry out their largest SAF operation yet in Latin America
title: Kuehne+Nagel, LATAM Cargo, and The Elite Flower carry out their largest SAF operation ...
description:
“Collaborating with our partners generates and drives positive and innovative changes in important industries like perishables and air logistics, where reducing carbon emissions is essential.
"This agreement stems from a shared conviction: managing emissions from the aviation industry requires multiple solutions and, above all, collaboration.
Together with our customers, we have taken another step towards more sustainable aviation by applying the environmental benefits of using Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) in the flower supply chain.
The incorporation of SAF into our logistics processes enables us to advance in reducing the carbon footprint of air transport, without compromising the quality or timeliness of our flower deliveries.
This type of initiative reflects our contribution to a more sustainable floriculture industry, where operational efficiency and environmental care go hand in hand.”
text:
“Collaborating with our partners generates and drives positive and innovative changes in important industries like perishables and air logistics, where reducing carbon emissions is essential. We are proud of the continued commitment to expand this initiative year after year and to inspire more stakeholders in Latin America and globally to continue advancing decarbonisation initiatives in our supply chains through Book and Claim methodology,” explains Ana San Carlos, Sustainability Manager for Latin America at Kuehne+Nagel.

"This agreement stems from a shared conviction: managing emissions from the aviation industry requires multiple solutions and, above all, collaboration. Together with our customers, we have taken another step towards more sustainable aviation by applying the environmental benefits of using Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) in the flower supply chain. The initiative demonstrates how reducing emissions further enhances the speed and reliability of air transport, which is key for fresh products such as flowers," said Cristina Oñate, VP of Sustainability and Product at LATAM Cargo Group.

Alvaro Camacho, Logistics Manager at The Elite Flower, commented that during the Valentine’s season, “We export nearly 40 million stems through LATAM Cargo, an operational challenge that we undertake with a commitment to doing it in an increasingly responsible way. The incorporation of SAF into our logistics processes enables us to advance in reducing the carbon footprint of air transport, without compromising the quality or timeliness of our flower deliveries. This type of initiative reflects our contribution to a more sustainable floriculture industry, where operational efficiency and environmental care go hand in hand.”

text:
Europe Aerospace Coatings Market Size

The Europe aerospace coatings market size was valued at USD 0.91 billion in 2025 and is anticipated to reach USD 0.95 billion in 2026 to USD 1.34 billion by 2034, growing at a CAGR of 4.77% during the forecast period from 2026 to 2034.

Aerospace coatings are specialized protective and functional surface treatments applied to aircraft structures, engines, and components to enhance durability, corrosion resistance, aerodynamic efficiency, and aesthetic appeal. In Europe, this market is tightly aligned with the continent’s civil aviation recovery, defense modernization, and sustainability mandates under the European Green Deal. These coatings must meet stringent certification standards set by the European Union Aviation Safety Agency and perform under extreme thermal, UV, and mechanical stress. As per the European Commission, thousands of commercial aircraft were registered in EU member states in 2024, each requiring periodic recoating during maintenance cycles. Additionally, the European Defence Agency confirmed that European NATO members operated a significant number of military fixed wing aircraft in 2024, many undergoing mid-life upgrades that include advanced coating systems. The shift toward sustainable aviation has intensified demand for low volatile organic compound formulations and thermally insulating topcoats that reduce fuel burn. Europe’s aerospace manufacturing base hosts leading airframers such as Airbus, which delivered hundreds of commercial aircraft in 2024 according to company disclosures, which is directly driving baseline coating consumption. This confluence of regulatory, operational, and environmental imperatives defines the unique trajectory of the Europe aerospace coatings market.

MARKET DRIVERS

Sustained Expansion of Commercial Aircraft Fleet and MRO Activity

The steady growth in Europe’s commercial aircraft fleet and associated maintenance, repair, and overhaul operations is a primary driver for of the European aerospace coatings market. As per Eurocontrol, European airspace handled millions of commercial flights in 2024, surpassing pre‑pandemic levels and necessitating frequent airframe inspections and repaints. Airbus reported a substantial backlog of aircraft at year end 2024, with European carriers such as Lufthansa, Air France KLM, and Ryanair accounting for a notable share of orders. Each narrow body aircraft typically undergoes a full exterior repaint every 7 to 10 years, consuming large volumes of aerospace coatings per cycle, according to the European Business Aviation Association. Furthermore, the European Union Aviation Safety Agency mandates corrosion protection inspections every 24 months for aircraft operating in coastal or high humidity regions as these conditions are prevalent in Southern Europe and the British Isles. This regulatory rhythm ensures recurring coating application irrespective of new build rates. Lufthansa Technik’s 2024 annual update highlighted an increase in full aircraft repaints compared to earlier years, underscoring the scale of MRO driven demand. As fleet utilisation intensifies and aircraft age profiles extend, the coating replacement cycle becomes both more frequent and more technically demanding, anchoring long term market resilience.

Defense Modernization and Next Generation Military Platform Deployment

Europe’s accelerated defense procurement and next generation aircraft programs are catalysing demand for advanced aerospace coatings with stealth, thermal, and radar absorbing properties, which is further supporting the expansion of the European aerospace coatings market. According to the European Defence Agency, defense expenditure among EU member states reached high levels in 2024, with aerospace systems comprising a significant portion of procurement budgets. Programs such as France and Germany’s Future Combat Air System, the UK’s Tempest fighter, and Sweden’s Gripen E upgrades require specialized coatings that reduce infrared signatures and radar cross sections. Saab confirmed in 2024 that each Gripen E airframe integrates extensive radar absorbing material-based topcoats, which are reapplied during depot level maintenance. Similarly, Dassault Aviation’s Rafale F4 standard includes new low emissivity coatings to enhance survivability in contested airspace. NATO’s Defence Investment Pledge further commits members to modernize a portion of their fleets by 2030, translating to hundreds of aircraft upgrades across Europe. These platforms demand multi-functional coatings that simultaneously provide corrosion resistance, electromagnetic shielding, and thermal management as these capabilities that command premium pricing and technical exclusivity. This strategic shift from legacy to fifth generation readiness is transforming aerospace coatings from a maintenance commodity into a mission critical technology layer.

MARKET RESTRAINTS

Stringent Environmental Regulations Limiting Use of Traditional Solvent Based Formulations

The European Union’s progressive chemical regulations, particularly REACH and the Industrial Emissions Directive, impose severe restrictions on hazardous substances commonly found in legacy aerospace coatings, including chromates, isocyanates, and high volatile organic compound solvents, which is restraining the growth of the European aerospace coatings market. As per the European Chemicals Agency, numerous coating related substances have been added to the REACH Candidate List since 2020, forcing formulators to reformulate products or seek costly authorizations. Hexavalent chromium, once standard in primers for its superior corrosion inhibition, is now effectively banned except under tightly controlled military exemptions. For instance, Airbus reported in 2024 that transitioning its A320 and A350 lines to chromate free primers increased material costs and required requalification of hundreds of coating processes across its European supply chain. Additionally, the EU’s Solvents Emissions Directive caps VOC content in aerospace coatings significantly below historical levels. Compliance necessitates investment in water based or high solids technologies, which often underperform in adhesion and weathering resistance. These regulatory hurdles delay certification, inflate R&D budgets, and limit the availability of drop-in replacements, thereby constraining coating performance and raising total lifecycle costs for both OEMs and MRO providers.

Extended Aircraft Production and Delivery Timelines Disrupt Coating Procurement Cycles

Supply chain bottlenecks and production delays across Europe’s aerospace sector have created misalignment between coating manufacturing schedules and actual aircraft assembly or maintenance activity, which is further impeding the regional market expansion. According to Airbus, shortages in engines and avionics pushed average delivery times for certain models higher in 2024 compared to earlier years. This backlog compression causes uneven demand spikes, where coating suppliers face sudden surges followed by prolonged lulls, undermining production efficiency. Similarly, the UK Civil Aviation Authority recorded an increase in aircraft storage at airports such as Teruel and Shannon in 2023, where many commercial jets remained grounded awaiting components. Stored aircraft still require protective coatings to prevent corrosion, but these are temporary and do not generate recurring revenue like full exterior systems. Moreover, deferred maintenance due to labor shortages further disrupts the repaint cycle. Coating manufacturers must therefore maintain inventory without predictable offtake, increasing working capital pressure. This operational volatility complicates long term planning and discourages investment in capacity expansion, particularly for smaller European coating formulators lacking global diversification.

MARKET OPPORTUNITIES

Integration of Smart and Multifunctional Coatings for Digital Aircraft Systems

The convergence of aerospace engineering and digital technology is creating demand for smart coatings that embed sensors that enable structural health monitoring, or support wireless communication, which is a major opportunity in the European aerospace coatings market. European research initiatives such as the Clean Sky 2 Joint Undertaking have funded projects developing conductive polymer coatings that detect micro cracks through electrical impedance changes. As per the German Aerospace Center, such coatings were successfully tested on an A320 testbed in 2024, reducing inspection time significantly. Airbus is also exploring anti icing coatings with embedded heating elements powered by the aircraft’s electrical system, eliminating the need for pneumatic bleed air and improving fuel efficiency according to internal trials. Furthermore, the European Aviation Safety Agency’s certification guidance now includes pathways for functional coatings that contribute to airworthiness, accelerating adoption. With the European Commission allocating substantial funding to smart materials under Horizon Europe 2021–2027, coating suppliers partnering with aerospace OEMs can transition from passive protectors to active system enablers. This technological leap not only commands premium pricing but also creates long term service contracts for coating monitoring and recalibration, opening recurring revenue streams beyond initial application.

Growth in Sustainable Aviation Fuel Compatible and Low Drag Coating Systems

The European Union’s ReFuelEU Aviation mandate that requires progressive sustainable aviation fuel blending, which is indirectly boosting demand for coatings that enhance fuel efficiency and compatibility with bio derived fuels and is another potential opportunity in the European aerospace coatings market. Sustainable aviation fuels exhibit different thermal and chemical properties than conventional Jet A1, accelerating degradation of standard topcoats. As per the European Committee for Standardization, new specifications for fuel resistant coatings were published in 2024, prompting Airbus and Boeing to qualify next generation polyurethane systems. Simultaneously, low drag coatings such as AkzoNobel’s Aerobase, which reduces skin friction, are gaining traction. Independent wind tunnel tests at ONERA in France confirmed that such coatings on a long-haul aircraft could deliver significant fuel savings annually. With European airlines committing to net zero emissions by 2050 under the Destination 2050 initiative, every percentage point of aerodynamic improvement is critical. As per the European Environment Agency, widespread adoption of drag reducing coatings could cut EU aviation CO2 emissions substantially by 2030. This regulatory and operational push is transforming aerospace coatings from aesthetic finishes into essential tools for climate compliance, unlocking new value propositions centred on performance sustainability.

MARKET CHALLENGES

High Certification Costs and Prolonged Qualification Timelines for New Coating Formulations

Introducing a new aerospace coating in Europe requires exhaustive testing and certification under EASA Part 21 and OEM‑specific standards, which is a process that typically spans years and costs millions of euros per formulation, as per the European Coatings Federation. Tests include extended salt spray exposure, UV cycling, and flammability assessments under FAA and EASA joint protocols. For example, PPG’s launch of its DESOTHANE CA8100 topcoat required extensive validation across Airbus, Boeing, and Leonardo platforms before approval. Smaller European coating innovators often lack the capital to sustain such timelines, ceding innovation to multinational players. Moreover, OEMs are reluctant to requalify existing fleets due to the risk of color mismatch or adhesion failure, preferring to restrict new coatings to new build aircraft. This creates a “certification lock in” where legacy formulations persist despite superior alternatives. As per the European Aviation Safety Agency, the backlog of material certification requests grew in 2024, further delaying market entry. Without harmonized EU wide fast track pathways for environmentally superior coatings, the market remains innovation averse, stifling the adoption of safer and more efficient chemistries despite clear performance and sustainability benefits.

Geopolitical Fragmentation and Divergent National Defense Specifications

Europe’s aerospace coatings market is hampered by fragmented defense procurement policies that require country specific coating formulations for military aircraft, undermining economies of scale and complicating supply chains. France mandates stealth coatings compliant with DGA standards, Germany adheres to BWB specifications, and the UK uses DEF STAN protocols. As per the European Defence Agency, these divergent specifications necessitate multiple qualification processes for a single coating intended for multinational platforms like the Eurofighter Typhoon. In 2024, the program’s coating supplier reported higher logistics costs due to maintaining parallel inventory lines for each partner nation. Furthermore, export controls on radar absorbing materials restrict cross border technology transfer, limiting collaborative development. Sweden’s SAAB must use domestically sourced coatings for Gripen exports to non-NATO countries due to restrictions, reducing supplier options. This balkanization increases lead times, inflates costs, and discourages SME participation. Although the European Defence Fund aims to harmonize standards, progress remains slow; as of 2025 only a small share of material specifications has been aligned under the European Defence Standardization Roadmap. Until interoperability improves, the defense segment will remain a high barrier niche, constraining market consolidation and innovation diffusion across the European aerospace coatings ecosystem.

REPORT COVERAGE

SEGMENTAL ANALYSIS

By Resin Insights

The epoxy resins segment dominated the market by holding 47.4% of the regional market share in 2025. The growth of the epoxy resins segment in the European market is driven by their exceptional adhesion, chemical resistance, and role as the primary corrosion inhibiting primer in multi-layer coating systems on both airframes and engine components. Epoxy based primers form the foundational layer on most commercial aircraft produced in Europe, including Airbus A320, A330, and A350 models, as confirmed by Airbus’s Surface Engineering Handbook. Their ability to withstand extended salt spray exposure makes them indispensable in marine and high humidity operating environments common across Southern Europe and transatlantic routes. Furthermore, epoxy systems are compatible with chromate free corrosion inhibitors mandated under EU REACH Annex XIV for civil applications. As per the German Aerospace Center, epoxy primers with nano silica reinforcement demonstrated improved fatigue crack resistance on aluminum substrates, extending inspection intervals. Despite environmental pressures, the segment’s technical indispensability in ensuring structural integrity ensures its continued primacy across both OEM and MRO applications.

The polyurethane resins segment represents the fastest growing resin segment in the Europe aerospace coatings market and is estimated to witness a CAGR of 6.6% over the forecast period. The dual role that polyurethane resins as topcoats that deliver aesthetic finish, UV stability, and emerging aerodynamic functionality is primarily fuelling the growth of the polyurethane resins segment in this regional market. Unlike epoxy, polyurethane directly interfaces with atmospheric conditions, requiring superior gloss retention and erosion resistance. Airbus mandates strict gloss retention benchmarks, which high solids polyurethanes consistently achieve. More critically, next generation low drag polyurethanes such as AkzoNobel’s Aerobase reduce skin friction, translating to annual fuel savings per long haul aircraft, as validated by ONERA wind tunnel tests. As per the European Environment Agency, widespread adoption could cut aviation CO2 emissions significantly by 2030. Additionally, the shift toward water reducible polyurethanes has overcome historical environmental barriers. With Airbus and Dassault Aviation qualifying new polyurethane systems in 2024, the segment is transitioning from cosmetic to performance critical, fueling its rapid adoption.

By Technology Insights

The solvent borne technology segment led the market by capturing 61.2% of the European market share in 2025. The growth of the solvent borne technology segment in the European market is driven by their superior film formation, adhesion on composite substrates, and compatibility with legacy application infrastructure across Europe’s MRO facilities. Solvent borne epoxy primers and polyurethane topcoats remain the standard for Airbus and Leonardo aircraft due to their ability to cure at ambient temperatures, which is a critical advantage in hangar environments lacking thermal ovens. As per Lufthansa Technik’s maintenance protocol, most repaint operations still rely on solvent borne systems because they achieve required thickness in fewer coats, reducing labor time compared to water borne alternatives. Additionally, solvent borne coatings exhibit lower sensitivity to humidity during application, a decisive factor in coastal maintenance hubs such as Toulouse, Marseille, and Hamburg. Although EU regulations restrict volatile organic compound content, high solids solvent formulations have been engineered to comply with VOC ceilings while maintaining performance. This balance of regulatory compliance, process efficiency, and field reliability sustains solvent borne dominance despite environmental headwinds.

The water borne coatings segment is the fastest growing technology segment in the Europe aerospace market and is expected to register a CAGR of 8.02% over the forecast period. The mounting regulatory pressure and OEM sustainability mandates targeting volatile organic compound emissions are propelling the expansion of the water borne coatings segment in the European market. The EU’s Industrial Emissions Directive now compels operators to adopt low emission alternatives. PPG’s AEROCRON water borne basecoat system, approved by Airbus, reduces VOC emissions significantly compared to conventional solvent systems while maintaining color match accuracy critical for airline branding. In 2024, major airline groups committed to sourcing only MRO providers using water borne coatings by 2027, influencing numerous European maintenance hubs. Moreover, advances in resin design have overcome historical drawbacks; as per the German Aerospace Center, new water borne epoxies achieve salt spray resistance close to solvent equivalents. With the European Environment Agency estimating that full adoption could eliminate thousands of metric tons of VOCs annually from aviation, water borne technology is transitioning from niche to mainstream, especially in new build OEM lines where process control mitigates application challenges.

By End User Insights

The OEM segment led the market by commanding for 56.5% of the European market share in 2025. The dominance of OEM segment in the European market is driven by Europe’s status as the global hub for commercial aircraft manufacturing, centered on Airbus’s final assembly lines in Toulouse, Hamburg, and Seville. Each Airbus aircraft requires large volumes of aerospace coatings across primer, basecoat, and topcoat layers. OEM applications demand the highest performance standards, with coatings integrated into the initial build process under tightly controlled clean room conditions that ensure uniform thickness and adhesion. Unlike MRO, OEM systems are co engineered with composite and metallic substrates, enabling synergistic performance. Airbus’s specification update in 2024 mandated chromate free epoxy primers on all new builds, which is triggering a continent-wide reformulation effort among coating suppliers. Furthermore, OEM contracts typically span many years, providing stable revenue and enabling long term R&D collaboration. This integration depth, scale, and technical rigor solidify the OEM segment as the cornerstone of European aerospace coatings consumption.

The MRO segment is the fastest growing end user category in the Europe aerospace coatings market and is anticipated to grow at a notable CAGR in the European market over the forecast period owing to the aging European commercial fleet, rising flight hours, and mandatory repaint cycles. As of 2024, a significant share of aircraft registered in EU member states were over 15 years old, according to Eurostat’s aviation registry. Each aircraft requires a full exterior repaint every 7 to 10 years, consuming large volumes of coatings per event. Lufthansa Technik reported performing fuller repaints in 2024 compared to earlier years, driven by post pandemic fleet reactivation. Additionally, corrosion under insulation on older jets necessitates spot recoating every 24 months as mandated by EASA Part M. The expansion of MRO hubs in Portugal, Spain, and Eastern Europe has increased coating application capacity since 2022, as per the European Regions Airline Association. Unlike OEM work, MRO coatings must perform across diverse aircraft types and environmental exposures, demanding formulation versatility. This operational complexity, combined with regulatory compliance and airline branding requirements, is transforming MRO into a high value growth engine for advanced coating systems.

COUNTRY ANALYSIS

France Aerospace Coatings Market Analysis

France held the leading position in the European aerospace coatings market in 2025 by holding 23.8% of the regional market share. The dominance of France in the European market is anchored in Toulouse, home to Airbus’s largest final assembly line, which consumes significant volumes of aerospace coatings monthly, primarily high-performance epoxy primers and polyurethane topcoats certified under Airbus AIMS standards. Safran’s engine facilities further drive demand for thermal barrier and anti-corrosion coatings resistant to extreme temperatures. France’s regulatory environment accelerates innovation; the Ministry of Ecological Transition mandated VOC reductions in aerospace coatings by 2025, spurring PPG and AkzoNobel to establish R&D centers near Toulouse. In 2024, ONERA validated a new silica nanoparticle reinforced polyurethane that reduces drag, now undergoing qualification on Airbus platforms. With billions of euros allocated to aerospace under France 2030, including green hydrogen powered coating lines, the country remains the continent’s technological and volume leader in aerospace surface engineering.

Germany Aerospace Coatings Market Analysis

Germany had the second biggest share of the European aerospace coatings market in 2025. The position of Germany in the European market is driven by its dense network of MRO providers and defense integrators. Lufthansa Technik’s facilities performed numerous aircraft repaints in 2024, while MTU Aero Engines applies specialized coatings to thousands of military and civil engines annually. Germany’s defense modernization further fuels demand; the Luftwaffe’s Eurofighter Typhoons are undergoing mid-life upgrades that include radar absorbing polyurethane topcoats compliant with national specifications. The German Aerospace Center’s tests confirmed these coatings reduce radar cross section in X band frequencies. Environmental regulations are equally influential; the German Federal Environment Agency enforced stricter VOC limits than EU norms, accelerating adoption of water borne epoxies. With the German government investing billions in aerospace R&D through 2026, including smart coating integration for structural health monitoring, Germany’s dual focus on civil maintenance and defense ensures sustained coating consumption across performance and compliance dimensions.

United Kingdom Aerospace Coatings Market Analysis

The United Kingdom is estimated to account for a prominent share of the European aerospace coatings market during the forecast period. The country’s position is defined by its leadership in military aerospace and the ongoing transition of civil MRO post Brexit. BAE Systems applies stealth and infrared suppressing coatings to Eurofighter Typhoon and Future Combat Air System prototypes, while Rolls Royce consumes high temperature ceramic topcoats for turbine blades. On the civil side, STS Aviation Services and British Airways Engineering processed numerous wide bodies repaints in 2024, though Brexit introduced customs delays for coating imports, prompting AkzoNobel and PPG to establish UK based blending units. The UK’s Aviation Strategy targets net zero emissions by 2050, driving trials of low drag coatings on British Airways’ A350 fleet, which showed measurable fuel reductions as validated by Imperial College London. These strategic shifts reinforce the UK’s niche in high value defense coatings while adapting its civil segment to new trade realities.

Spain Aerospace Coatings Market Analysis

Spain is projected to grow at a healthy CAGR in the European aerospace coatings market over the forecast period. The country’s aerospace coatings demand is bifurcated between Airbus’s A350 final assembly line near Madrid and a rapidly expanding MRO sector in Seville, Teruel, and Reus. The Illescas facility completed numerous A350 aircraft in 2024, each requiring large volumes of chromate free epoxy and polyurethane coatings. Simultaneously, Teruel Airport hosted hundreds of commercial jets in 2024, many undergoing corrosion protection or full repaints. Spanish MRO providers have partnered with European coating suppliers to develop humidity resistant formulations suitable for the Mediterranean climate. The Spanish government’s PERTE Aerospace program allocated billions of euros through 2025, including grants for automated spray booths that reduce coating waste. With Iberia Express and Vueling expanding fleets and mandating regular repaints for brand consistency, Spain’s dual OEM MRO ecosystem is driving steady coating uptake in Southern Europe.

Italy Aerospace Coatings Market Analysis

Italy is expected to command for a notable share of the European aerospace coatings market during the forecast period. The country’s demand is shaped by Leonardo’s military aircraft production in Turin and regional airline operations. Leonardo delivered trainer jets and helicopters in 2024, each requiring radar transparent and UV stable topcoats for training and maritime roles. Alenia Aermacchi’s M‑346, used by air forces in multiple countries, features specialized polyurethane systems validated by the Italian Air Force’s durability trials. On the civil side, ITA Airways and Neos operate fleets requiring repaints every several years, with MRO work concentrated at Rome Fiumicino and Milan Malpensa. Italy’s mountainous terrain and coastal operations necessitate enhanced corrosion protection as ENAC mandates additional primer layers for aircraft based in Sicily and Sardinia due to salt laden air. The Italian National Recovery Plan allocated significant funding to aerospace modernization, including coating lines for unmanned aerial vehicles at the Torino Aerotech Park. This blend of defense specificity and regional environmental adaptation sustains Italy’s strategic role in Europe’s coating supply chain.

COMPETITIVE LANDSCAPE

The Europe aerospace coatings market exhibits highly competitive intensity characterized by technological differentiation stringent certification barriers and deep integration with OEM and MRO ecosystems. Competition is dominated by multinational chemical companies that leverage global R&D networks to meet Europe’s exacting environmental and performance standards. While price plays a role the primary battleground is regulatory compliance product certification cycle time and technical service capability. New entrants face significant hurdles due to the 18-to-36-month qualification timelines imposed by EASA and airframers which favor established players with proven track records. However, the push toward sustainability is creating openings for innovative formulations particularly in water borne and low drag segments. Defense applications add another layer of complexity through national specification fragmentation and export controls. Overall, the market rewards companies that combine chemical innovation with localized support and strategic alignment with Europe’s decarbonization and defense autonomy agendas fostering a dynamic yet consolidated competitive environment.

Top Players In The Market

AkzoNobel is a global leader in performance coatings with a strong foothold in the European aerospace sector through its Aerocoat and Aerobase product lines. The company supplies advanced epoxy primers and low drag polyurethane topcoats to major OEMs including Airbus and Leonardo. In 2024 AkzoNobel launched a next generation water borne basecoat system certified for use on Airbus A320neo and A350 aircraft which reduces volatile organic compound emissions by over 75 percent while maintaining color consistency. The company also established a dedicated aerospace innovation center in Sassenheim Netherlands to accelerate development of smart and sustainable coatings aligned with EU Green Deal objectives. These initiatives reinforce its role as a technology partner in decarbonizing aviation through surface engineering.

PPG Industries maintains a prominent presence in the European aerospace coatings market through its DESOTHANE and AEROCRON product families which serve both OEM and MRO segments. The company collaborates closely with Airbus Lufthansa Technik and Safran to deliver chromate free primers and high durability topcoats that meet EASA and OEM specifications. In 2023 PPG received Airbus approval for its AEROCRON water borne coating system now deployed across multiple European MRO facilities. Additionally, the company expanded its blending and distribution center in Villeparisis France to support just in time delivery of customized colors and formulations. These actions enhance PPG’s responsiveness to regulatory shifts and airline branding demands while strengthening its supply chain resilience in continental Europe.

Henkel AG contributes to the European aerospace coatings landscape through its technologically advanced surface treatment and primer solutions under the BONDERITE and TEROSON brands. While less visible in topcoats Henkel plays a critical role in pre-treatment and corrosion protection layers applied before final coating. In 2024 the company introduced a new zirconium based non chromate conversion coating validated by Airbus for use on aluminum and composite substrates reducing hazardous waste by 90 percent compared to traditional processes. Henkel also deepened its partnership with MTU Aero Engines to develop thermal barrier coatings for next generation low emission turbines. By focusing on upstream surface engineering Henkel ensures adhesion integrity and longevity of downstream coatings solidifying its strategic value in Europe’s integrated aerospace manufacturing ecosystem.

Top Strategies Used By The Key Market Participants

Key players in the Europe aerospace coatings market are prioritizing sustainability through the development of water borne and high solids formulations that comply with stringent EU volatile organic compound regulations. Companies are investing in chromate free corrosion protection technologies to align with REACH restrictions while maintaining performance parity. Strategic collaboration with OEMs like Airbus and defense integrators enables co engineered solutions tailored to specific airframes and missions. Expansion of local blending and distribution hubs across France Germany and Spain enhances supply chain agility and color customization capabilities. Additionally, firms are advancing smart coating technologies that integrate structural health monitoring or drag reduction features supported by EU funded research initiatives. These strategies collectively address environmental compliance operational efficiency and next generation performance requirements across civil and defense aviation segments.

title: Air France-KLM warns EU climate rules would halve Asia flights - Financial Times
description:
Benjamin Smith, chief executive of the airline, said regulations requiring EU airlines to use increasing amounts of expensive sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) would hobble EU airlines’ ability to compete on routes from Europe into Asia.
To level the playing field, Smith and other EU airline industry chiefs have called for the commission to introduce a scheme, dubbed an SAF Border Adjustment Mechanism, or “SAF-BAM”.
It would require non-EU airlines to purchase certificates equivalent to the value of the SAF used by EU airlines.
“This approach offers a pragmatic way to uphold ambitious SAF targets — critical for driving investment — while safeguarding the competitiveness of EU airlines and hubs,” he said.
Brussels is looking into the risks of European airlines being undercut by more polluting rivals, but discussions are at an early stage, one official said.
text:
Air France-KLM has warned it will be forced to cut almost half its flights to Asia if the EU does not pare back climate policies that leave the industry at a “significant competitive disadvantage” against rivals.

Benjamin Smith, chief executive of the airline, said regulations requiring EU airlines to use increasing amounts of expensive sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) would hobble EU airlines’ ability to compete on routes from Europe into Asia.

“European airlines face a significant competitive disadvantage, largely due to Europe’s reluctance to address persistent market distortions,” Smith told the FT. “This imbalance not only jeopardises our climate ambitions but also threatens the competitiveness of European aviation.”

Rising requirements for SAF would render eight of its 13 routes to Asia unprofitable within a decade, the airline forecast. The company has already closed five routes since 2015, because of rising costs that rivals in the Gulf do not face.

European airlines have already ceded market share on Asian routes to rivals such as Emirates, Qatar or Turkish Airlines, which connect passengers through their central hubs in Dubai, Doha or Istanbul.

Flag carriers such as British Airways and Lufthansa have also cut Asian routes. Long-haul journeys are typically the most profitable for the established operators.

Under the bloc’s plan to reduce carbon emissions from the aviation industry, EU airlines have had to include 2 per cent SAF in their fuels since January 1 2025 — rising to 6 per cent in 2030 and 20 per cent by 2035. The UK has similar targets.

Non-EU carriers that split journeys to Asia via hubs in the Middle East can avoid a large proportion of the higher costs imposed by using SAF.

Since non-European rivals flying via a non-EU hub avoid SAF and emission trading costs on the second leg of the journey, “we risk losing up to two-thirds of long-haul passengers to our competitors,” warned Ourania Georgoutsakou, managing director of A4E, the industry lobby.

According to a report published in October by the European Union Aviation Safety Agency, the average price of SAF in 2024 was €2,085 per tonne compared with €734 per tonne for conventional jet fuel.

To level the playing field, Smith and other EU airline industry chiefs have called for the commission to introduce a scheme, dubbed an SAF Border Adjustment Mechanism, or “SAF-BAM”.

It would require non-EU airlines to purchase certificates equivalent to the value of the SAF used by EU airlines. Certificates would be benchmarked to the market price of SAF, with an offset to take into consideration any local SAF mandates.

French liberal MEP Pascal Canfin, a former chair of the European parliament’s environment committee, said that a SAF-BAM was necessary to avoid undermining the bloc’s wider ambitions on tackling climate change.

“This approach offers a pragmatic way to uphold ambitious SAF targets — critical for driving investment — while safeguarding the competitiveness of EU airlines and hubs,” he said.

The EU’s airlines are also facing a potential extension of the bloc’s emissions trading system to flights leaving European airports, pending the results of a review due later this year.

Modelling by consultancy Deloitte for A4E, the industry lobby, estimated that by 2030 a flight from Nice to Tokyo would be 15 per cent cheaper for non-EU carriers flying to Asia via hubs such as Istanbul or Dubai.

Gulf airports are already expanding in anticipation of increasing traffic between Europe and Asia.

Earlier this week, Bahrain announced a partnership with Malaysia’s Air Asia X, a low-cost operator that plans to begin services between Kuala Lumpur and London Gatwick, using the Gulf airport as a transfer point.

The kingdom wants to become “a preferred destination for airlines, whether commercial airlines or private aviation,” Bahrain’s minister for transportation, Shaikh Abdulla bin Ahmed Al Khalifa, told the FT.

Sites across the region are expanding. Istanbul’s main airport has plans to put in three more runways by 2037, giving it capacity for 150mn passengers a year.

The airport’s chief executive Selahattin Bilgen said the appeal of the airport, which rivals Heathrow in passenger numbers, was its central position globally, as well as Turkey’s booming tourism industry.

He claimed the country had its own SAF mandates that were close to European levels.

Despite this, the EU airline industry is stepping up pressure on the European Commission to take steps to reduce the effects of the EU’s unilateral SAF mandate on their future competitiveness, particularly on routes to Asia.

Brussels is looking into the risks of European airlines being undercut by more polluting rivals, but discussions are at an early stage, one official said.

A4E boss Georgoutsakou said the industry needed a “basket of measures” to safeguard competitiveness: “We need to level the global playing field.”
